title: FCAS Collapses, F-35 Readiness Hits New Low, and the Strait of Hormuz Gets a Demining Plan
subhead: Europe's flagship fighter program is officially dead, only 1 in 4 US F-35s is fully mission-capable, and Britain and France finalize a 15-nation coalition to clear Iranian mines from Hormuz — while Taiwan fires HIMARS toward China for the first time.

sections.heading: FCAS Fighter Program Collapses as Germany and France Pull the Plug
sections.body: Europe's most ambitious defense cooperation project is over. Germany and France have formally terminated the €100 billion Future Combat Air System (FCAS) program after Chancellor Friedrich Merz and President Emmanuel Macron concluded at a summit in Montenegro on June 8 that disputes between Dassault Aviation and Airbus were 'irreconcilable.' The program, launched in 2017 to produce a sixth-generation fighter by 2040, foundered on leadership roles, work-share distribution, and technical design authority. Germany and Spain will continue developing a fighter without France, while France will pursue its own path. Berlin's air force chief said at the Berlin Air Show that Germany will need to 'pick up a fifth-gen-plus system by 2035,' signaling likely additional F-35 orders. The non-fighter elements of FCAS, including the 'combat cloud' networking system, may survive. The collapse draws inevitable comparisons to the UK-led Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP) with Italy and Japan, which remains intact.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe now has two competing sixth-gen fighter efforts instead of one unified program, driving up costs and fragmentation at exactly the wrong moment. Germany's likely pivot to more F-35s deepens reliance on American platforms, undermining the strategic autonomy both Paris and Berlin claim to want.

sections.heading: Only 1 in 4 US F-35s Fully Mission-Capable as Readiness Declines Further
sections.body: The US Government Accountability Office reports that the F-35 fleet's full mission capable rate fell to 25% through fiscal year 2025, continuing a multi-year decline. The world's costliest weapons program, the F-35 has seen readiness rates drop despite years of sustainment investment. The Pentagon is now seeking an additional $13.7 billion to boost sustainment, according to Breaking Defense. The readiness figure means that at any given time, three-quarters of the US F-35 fleet cannot perform all assigned missions — a striking gap as allies in Europe and the Indo-Pacific increase their reliance on the platform.
sections.whyItMatters: At a time when Germany may buy more F-35s to fill its FCAS gap and allies across NATO and Asia are betting their air superiority on the type, a 25% mission-capable rate raises hard questions about whether the fleet can deliver in a high-tempo conflict.

sections.heading: Britain and France Finalize 15-Nation Hormuz Demining Mission
sections.body: Britain and France have finalized plans to lead a multinational mine-clearing mission in the Strait of Hormuz involving 15 countries, according to Bloomberg/Straits Times reporting. The mission will deploy once the US and Iran reach an agreement to restore commercial navigation. Iran laid extensive naval mines in the strait via IRGC speedboats during the recent conflict, and while President Trump claimed US forces had 'gotten most of them,' Secretary of State Rubio told senators that Iran had mined large sections of the waterway. Britain's Royal Navy has already sent RFA Lyme Bay, loaded with autonomous mine-hunting systems, from Gibraltar toward the strait. Notably, the US has no purpose-built mine countermeasures ships in the region and is retiring its remaining four Avenger-class minesweepers. The Netherlands also redirected a frigate toward Hormuz on Friday, Reuters reports. Iran has indicated it wants to demine the strait itself, but Britain and France do not believe Tehran has the capability.
sections.whyItMatters: A 15-nation European-led demining operation in Hormuz represents a significant post-conflict security role for European navies, filling a capability gap the US cannot address with its own assets. Whether Iran accepts a Western-led sweep of the strait will be the next flashpoint.

sections.heading: Taiwan Fires HIMARS Toward China for the First Time
sections.body: Taiwan fired dozens of rockets from US-supplied HIMARS launchers toward China for the first time, launching from its western coast in a live-fire exercise around Taichung. The two-day drill simulated repelling an amphibious invasion along 'red beaches' — the most likely Chinese landing zones. While the 32 rockets tested had a range of only about nine miles, Taiwan possesses longer-range ATACMS-type munitions capable of reaching the Chinese mainland. The exercise also involved the indigenous Thunderbolt-2000 rocket system, Paladin howitzers, and anti-tank guided missiles. Analyst William Yang of Crisis Group called the western-coast HIMARS firing 'symbolically significant,' signaling Taiwan's determination and strike capability. The drills came less than a month after Trump referred to Taiwan as a 'negotiating chip' and his administration indefinitely paused a $14 billion weapons package to the island.
sections.whyItMatters: The first HIMARS firing toward China is a deliberate signal from Taipei at a moment when US commitment is uncertain. It demonstrates both operational proficiency and political resolve — and reminds Beijing that Taiwan's asymmetric capabilities are maturing even as Washington wavers on arms deliveries.

sections.heading: UK Seizes Russian Shadow Fleet Tanker in English Channel
sections.body: Royal Marine Commandos and National Crime Agency officers boarded the Smyrtos, a Russian shadow fleet tanker, in a six-hour helicopter-borne operation in the English Channel — the first such seizure by UK armed forces. The Cameroon-flagged vessel, sanctioned since July 2025, had sailed from Russia's Ust-Luga port on June 5. An Indian national, the ship's captain Ajay Pant, has been charged with contravening Russia sanctions. 24 Georgian and Indian crew members remain aboard the vessel, anchored off the Dorset coast. The MoD said the operation was conducted in international waters, more than 12 nautical miles from the UK coast, and was coordinated with France. Moscow described the seizure as 'bordering on international piracy.' The UK has sanctioned over 500 shadow fleet vessels.
sections.whyItMatters: This is an escalation in Western enforcement against Russia's oil sanctions-evasion fleet — from financial measures to physical military seizure. The coordination with France and the international-waters legal framing signals that further boardings are likely.

sections.heading: North Korea Declares Nuclear Status 'Irreversible' as Xi Visit Ends
sections.body: North Korea's Foreign Ministry declared that 'denuclearization is an issue that has been finally and irreversibly concluded', rejecting renewed calls from the G7, South Korea, and the EU. The statement followed a visit by Chinese President Xi Jinping to Pyongyang — the first by a Chinese leader in years — and the sixth meeting of the US-South Korea Nuclear Consultative Group. Pyongyang said it was considering 'military and technical countermeasures' and described South Korea as its 'foremost hostile state.' A separate statement issued under a new 'Department 10' designation criticized South Korean President Lee Jae Myung's denuclearization stance, prompting speculation about a reorganization of North Korea's inter-Korean messaging apparatus.
sections.whyItMatters: Xi's visit provided Pyongyang with diplomatic cover just before this declaration. The 'irreversible' language and the new Department 10 suggest North Korea is formalizing its nuclear status administratively — making future disarmament negotiations even less viable.

sections.heading: US Marines Plan Permanent Weapons Stockpile in Australia
sections.body: US Navy tender documents reveal advanced planning for a permanent Marine Corps weapons stockpile in Victoria, Australia, with US$30 million allocated to build warehouses and offices at Bandiana military base. The stockpile, expected to reach full capacity by 2028, would be the Marine Corps' first land-based pre-positioning site in the Asia-Pacific — complementing the floating maritime pre-positioning squadrons used since the Cold War. A separate Philippines-based stockpile is expected to open this year. The Victoria site was chosen because it sits beyond the range of most Chinese missiles, according to analysts cited by SCMP/AFP.
sections.whyItMatters: A permanent war-ready stockpile in southern Australia extends US Marine Corps reach into the South Pacific and adds resilience to the Indo-Pacific logistics chain — a practical investment in deterrence that doesn't require host-nation basing negotiations for combat troops.

bottomLine: Europe's defense integration took a body blow with the FCAS collapse, pushing Germany toward American F-35s even as the GAO reveals only a quarter of that fleet is mission-ready. Meanwhile, European navies are stepping into the Hormuz gap the US can't fill, Taiwan is signaling resolve with HIMARS as Washington wavers, and Britain has escalated from sanctioning shadow fleet tankers to physically seizing them. The hardware decisions being made this week — FCAS alternatives, F-35 sustainment, Hormuz demining, Australian pre-positioning — will shape alliance force structures for a decade.
sources: Breaking Defense, Crypto Briefing, SCMP/AFP, BBC, Reuters, Bloomberg/Straits Times, GAO, UPI/Asia Today, Anadolu Agency, Yahoo News/Telegraph, MSN, NBC News, Mirror, Barents Observer, Defense News
